answers.question: List of scenarios
answers.evidence: Scenario | Net Zero 2050 (Orderly Category) | Delayed Transition (Disorderly Category) | Current Policies (Hot House World Category) ... The sector group chose the following three Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) scenarios as the basis for the sector-level scenarios
answers.notes: Three NGFS-based scenarios were used, adapted for the New Zealand retail sector. A baseline assessment at 1.1°C was also conducted but is not a named scenario.

answers.question: Global temperature outcome by 2100
answers.answer: Net Zero 2050: 1.6°C by 2050, reducing to 1.4°C by 2100. Delayed Transition: 1.7°C by 2050, reducing to 1.6°C by 2100. Current Policies (Hot House World): 2°C by 2050 and more than 3°C by 2100. A baseline assessment at 1.1°C (current warming) was also used.
answers.evidence: An ambitious and coordinated transition to a low-emissions, climate resilient future. Stringent climate policies, innovation, ambitious investment, and medium-to-high deployment of carbon removal solutions limit global warming to 1.6°C in 2050 and reducing to 1.4°C by 2100. ... Ambitious action is delayed to 2030, followed by sudden and uncoordinated economic transformation. Extensive, stringent and punitive but late government intervention, in combination with some deployment of carbon removal solutions limit global warming to 1.7°C in 2050 and reducing to 1.6°C by 2100. ... Current emissions reduction policies are implemented. Current socio-economic trends continue, resulting in 2°C global warming by 2050 and more than 3°C by 2100.
answers.notes: Temperature outcomes are explicitly stated for each scenario at both 2050 and 2100 milestones. No temperature overshoot language is used.

answers.question: Scenario provider
answers.answer: NGFS (Network for Greening the Financial System)
answers.evidence: The sector group chose the following three Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) scenarios as the basis for the sector-level scenarios
answers.notes: NGFS is explicitly named as the source of the three scenarios used.

answers.question: Pick-and-mix of SSPs and RCPs
answers.evidence: The sector group chose the following three Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) scenarios as the basis for the sector-level scenarios
answers.notes: The document only references NGFS scenarios. No SSP or RCP identifiers are mentioned anywhere in the documents.

answers.question: Customised scenarios
answers.evidence: In the first half of the year, we collaborated with other retail industry participants to co-design a set of integrated climate change scenarios for New Zealand's retail sector. The sector group chose the following three Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) scenarios as the basis for the sector-level scenarios ... A retail sector narrative was formed for each scenario identifying the critical interactions and key outcomes and indicators.
answers.notes: The NGFS scenarios were adapted/customised into sector-level scenarios for New Zealand's retail sector, with retail-specific narratives developed for each scenario. The document explicitly states they were used "as the basis" for sector-level scenarios, indicating adaptation beyond off-the-shelf use.

answers.question: Co-produced climate scenarios
answers.evidence: In the first half of the year, we collaborated with other retail industry participants to co-design a set of integrated climate change scenarios for New Zealand's retail sector.
answers.notes: The document explicitly uses the term "co-design" and describes collaboration with other retail industry participants in developing the scenarios.

answers.question: Baseline scenarios
answers.answer: Yes. A baseline risk assessment representing 1.1°C of global warming was used to identify current physical and transition impacts already incurred.
answers.evidence: a baseline risk assessment representing 1.1°C of global warming helping us to identify the current physical and transition impacts we have incurred, and two further scenarios representing 1.5°C and 3.0°C of global warming.
answers.location_hint: Climate-Related Disclosures, p.2 (Risk Assessment process description)
answers.notes: The 1.1°C baseline represents current warming levels and is used as a reference point against which the 1.5°C and 3.0°C scenarios are compared.

answers.question: Model used
answers.notes: No specific climate models, integrated assessment models (IAMs), or macroeconomic models are named in either document. The documents reference NGFS scenarios and external experts but do not name any specific models.

answers.question: Damage from acute physical risk considered
answers.evidence: Increased storminess/ extreme winds River and pluvial flooding ... Increase in storminess (frequency, intensity) including tropical cyclones. Changes in extreme wind speed. Increase in convective weather events (tornadoes, lightning). Changes in extremes: high intensity and persistence of rainfall. Increase in hail severity or frequency. There will be an increasing incidence of storm events with increasing severity impacting supply chains and operations. Potential store closures. Delays in supply chain. Team and customers unable to get to our stores. Loss of sales and decrease in profit. Cost of repairs/maintenance to buildings. Increased lease costs. Increased supply chain costs. ... Global supply chain risk: A main international port is affected (e.g. by storms/or floods). ... Sea level rise of 0.32m could impact specific locations and increase losses due to flooding.
answers.location_hint: Climate-Related Disclosures, p.3-4 (Key Physical Risks table)
answers.notes: The document explicitly identifies acute physical risks (storms, flooding, cyclones) and describes their potential financial impacts including loss of sales, cost of repairs, and supply chain disruptions. Quantified sea level rise (0.32m) is also mentioned.

answers.question: Non-temperature variables in scenarios
answers.evidence: These scenarios considered three different time horizons: short (2023-2030), medium (2031-2040) and long (2041-2050) and explored the political, environmental, societal, technological, legal and economic impacts across each potential pathway. ... Increase in storminess (frequency, intensity) including tropical cyclones. Changes in extreme wind speed. Increase in convective weather events (tornadoes, lightning). Changes in extremes: high intensity and persistence of rainfall. Increase in hail severity or frequency. ... Relative sea-level rise (including land movement). Change in tidal range or increased water depth. Permanent increase in spring high-tide inundation. Rising groundwater from sea-level rise. Sea level rise of 0.32m could impact specific locations and increase losses due to flooding.
answers.location_hint: Climate-Related Disclosures, p.2-5 (Scenario descriptions and risk tables)
answers.notes: The scenarios explore political, environmental, societal, technological, legal and economic dimensions. Physical variables include storms, flooding, sea-level rise, and rainfall. Transition variables include policy/regulation, consumer preferences, and insurance.

answers.question: Scale of scenarios
answers.evidence: we collaborated with other retail industry participants to co-design a set of integrated climate change scenarios for New Zealand's retail sector. The sector group chose the following three Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) scenarios as the basis for the sector-level scenarios ... Sea level rise of 0.32m could impact specific locations and increase losses due to flooding. Work under way to identify at risk catchments.
answers.notes: The scenarios are based on global NGFS scenarios but have been adapted for New Zealand's retail sector context. Specific local impacts (e.g., sea level rise of 0.32m at specific locations, named NZ cities) suggest some localisation, though the document does not explicitly describe a formal downscaling methodology.

answers.question: Time frames
answers.answer: Two sets of time horizons are used: (1) Sector-based scenario time horizons: short (2023–2030), medium (2031–2040), long (2041–2050). (2) Entity-level business planning time horizons: short-term (1–3 years), medium-term (3–10 years), long-term (>10 years). The rationale for the entity-level timeframes is explained: they were aligned to Briscoe Group's own business planning and investment timeframes.
answers.evidence: These scenarios considered three different time horizons: short (2023-2030), medium (2031-2040) and long (2041-2050) ... The sector-based time horizons which look out to 2050 were used in the workshops to provide guidance however an important outcome of the workshops was to align risks and opportunities to entity level business planning and investment timeframes of: Short-term: one to three years; Medium-term: three to 10 years; Long term: > 10years
answers.notes: The rationale for the entity-level timeframes is explicitly stated as alignment with business planning and investment timeframes. No explicit rationale is given for the sector-based time horizons (2023–2050).

answers.question: Justification for scenario/model choice
answers.answer: Yes — partially. The document states that a combination of scenarios was used to add resilience to the risk assessment process, and that the NGFS scenarios were chosen as the basis for the sector-level scenarios by the retail sector group. However, no detailed justification is provided for why NGFS specifically was selected over other scenario frameworks.
answers.evidence: Using a combination of scenarios was intended to add resilience to the risk assessment process and the resultant strategy as we prepare for inevitable uncertainty in the short to medium-term.
answers.notes: The justification given is for using a combination of scenarios (resilience to uncertainty), not for the choice of NGFS as the scenario framework. No explicit rationale is provided for selecting NGFS over IPCC or other providers.
